description: User-controlled data flows into a SQL query without sanitization.

content.body: req.params.id is interpolated directly into the SQL string. An attacker can set id to e.g. '1 OR 1=1' to bypass WHERE clauses or '1; DROP TABLE users;--' to execute arbitrary statements. Use a parameterised query: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id]).

description: User-controlled data flows into a DELETE SQL query without sanitization.

content.body: The DELETE endpoint interpolates req.params.id without any validation or escaping. This allows privilege escalation (delete any row) or destructive injection. Replace with a parameterised statement and add authorisation checks.

description: Hard-coded cryptographic secret detected in source code.

content.body: The value 'hardcoded-secret-abc123' is committed to version control. Any developer, CI system, or attacker with repository access can extract it and forge tokens. Load secrets from environment variables (process.env.SECRET_KEY) or a secrets manager at runtime.

description: MD5 is a cryptographically broken hash algorithm.

content.body: MD5 is vulnerable to collision attacks and, for passwords, can be reversed via rainbow tables in seconds. Use a slow, salted password hashing function: bcrypt (cost ≥ 12), scrypt, or Argon2id.

description: Password value passed to console.log — credentials will appear in application logs.

content.body: Logging the raw password exposes credentials in log files, monitoring dashboards, and crash reports. Log only non-sensitive fields such as username and timestamp, and redact or omit the password entirely.

description: Authentication token generated without a cryptographic signature.

content.body: btoa(JSON.stringify(payload)) produces a base64-encoded string that any client can decode and modify without detection. Use a proper JWT library with a signing algorithm (HS256 with a strong secret, or RS256/ES256 with an asymmetric key pair).

description: DELETE endpoint performs a destructive operation without verifying caller identity.

content.body: The /user/:id DELETE route executes a database deletion without checking that the request is authenticated or that the caller is authorised to delete the target resource. Add an authentication middleware and a resource ownership check.

description: strcpy() is a banned API and a known source of stack-based buffer overflows.

content.body: strcpy() has been removed from secure coding standards (SEI CERT C STR31-C, CWE-120). Replace with strncpy(dst, src, dst_size - 1) followed by explicit null-termination, or use a safe string library.

description: memcpy() called with an attacker-controlled length — potential stack smash.

content.body: The 'size' parameter flows from the caller without bounds checking against buf[256]. Writing beyond the buffer boundary overwrites the return address, enabling stack-smashing exploits (CWE-121). Add: if ((size_t)size > sizeof(buf)) { /* handle error */ return; }

description: Potential integer overflow in malloc() size computation: n * sizeof(int).

content.body: If 'n' is attacker-controlled, the multiplication n * sizeof(int) can overflow a signed int, wrapping to a small positive value. malloc() then allocates a small buffer, and subsequent writes to arr[0..n-1] overflow the heap (CWE-122). Use size_t arithmetic and a checked multiply.

description: Heap memory freed in free_resources() remains accessible through 'global_buffer'.

content.body: After free(global_buffer), any code path that later dereferences a stale copy of the pointer triggers undefined behaviour. Heap use-after-free is a critical vulnerability (CWE-416) commonly exploited to achieve arbitrary code execution via heap spray or tcache poisoning.
